title: Testing Edge Cases
intro: Edge cases are the weird, unusual inputs that break your code -- empty strings, zero, null, negative numbers, very large numbers. Just like car manufacturers test vehicles in extreme heat and freezing cold, you need to test your code with extreme inputs. A developer's function passes all the normal tests but crashes on edge cases. Find the missing protection.

teach.title: Edge Cases = Extreme Weather Testing
teach.body: Car manufacturers don't just test cars in sunny weather. They test in extreme heat, freezing cold, heavy rain, and salt spray. **Edge cases** are the extreme conditions for your code. They're the weird inputs that users will definitely send -- empty forms, super long text, negative numbers, or no input at all.

teach.title: Edge Cases = Childproofing Your Home
teach.body: When you childproof a house, you don't just protect against normal use. You think: what if they pull this? What if they stick a finger in that? What if they try to eat it? **Testing edge cases** is childproofing your code. Users will do things you never imagined -- submit empty forms, paste 10,000 characters, enter negative ages. If you didn't test for it, they WILL find it.

teach.title: The Most Common Edge Cases
teach.body:
Always test these scenarios:

- **Empty/null**: What if the input is `null`, `undefined`, or `''`?
- **Zero**: Does your math work with 0? Division by zero?
- **Negative numbers**: Can quantity be -5?
- **Boundaries**: If max is 100, what about 99, 100, and 101?
- **Very large values**: What about 999999999?
- **Special characters**: What about input with emoji, quotes, or HTML?

teach.title: Testing Edge Cases
teach.annotation: Each test targets a specific edge case. These are the inputs users WILL eventually send, so your code needs to handle them.

teach.body: AI-generated code loves the happy path but often ignores the weird stuff. Edge case tests are your insurance policy -- they catch the bugs that make users leave a 1-star review and ask for a refund.
Edge cases are unusual inputs that break code: null, zero, empty strings, boundaries, and extreme values. Always test these -- they're where most real-world bugs hide.

exercise.prompt: This function calculates the average of an array of scores. It works for [90, 80, 70] but crashes when called with an empty array []. Find the line that causes the problem.
exercise.explanation: When the array is empty, scores.length is 0, so the function divides by zero: total / 0. In JavaScript, this returns NaN (Not a Number), not an error. The fix checks for an empty array first and returns 0. This is a classic edge case -- the function works perfectly for normal inputs but fails on the boundary case of an empty array.
